name: Monstera obliqua (Peru form)
origin: Native to tropical lowland rainforests of Peru, South America, as a hemiepiphyte.
aboutText: Monstera obliqua (Peru form) is native to the tropical lowland rainforests of Peru, South America, where it thrives as a delicate hemiepiphytic aroid. It prefers warm, humid environments with bright indirect light and airy support for its climbing habit. This particular form is among the most delicate of all Monstera species, characterized by its extremely thin, papery leaves that feature dramatic internal perforations and fine tissue bridges, often appearing more hole than leaf. Its slender petioles and stems support its growth, distinguishing it from species like Monstera adansonii by its finer texture and more fragile structure, making it a highly prized rarity for advanced collectors.

morphology.leafShape: Ovate to lanceolate, with dramatic perforations

propagation.notes: Requires particularly stable conditions during rooting. Node cuttings with one leaf are preferred. Mist frequently and maintain 80%+ humidity.

careGuide.substrate: Chunky, well-draining mix: 40% potting compost, 30% perlite, 20% orchid bark, 10% worm castings. Monsteras are more tolerant of moisture than most aroids but still need drainage.
careGuide.watering: Water when the top 3–5 cm is dry. These are forgiving plants but still susceptible to root rot if kept too wet. Water less in winter.
careGuide.humidity: 50–70% preferred. More tolerant of typical UK indoor humidity than other aroids, though 60%+ encourages larger leaf development.
careGuide.fertilising: Balanced fertiliser at half strength every 2 weeks from spring through summer. Monthly in winter or none at all.
careGuide.repotting: Every 12–18 months when roots emerge from drainage holes. Provide a moss pole or support — climbing encourages larger leaf production.

commonProblems.cause: Plant is juvenile, or insufficient light
commonProblems.fix: Ensure bright indirect light and allow the plant to mature — fenestrations develop with age and better conditions
Check soil moisture before watering; begin a regular fertilising routine in growing season

commonProblems.cause: Sunburn or cold water on leaves
commonProblems.fix: Avoid direct sun; water at the base and ensure water is room temperature

items: Ensure there is at least one healthy node present — a leaf without a node cannot produce new growth
For variegated forms, verify variegation is present on the newest leaf and is not reverting to green
Check for scale insects on petioles and undersides of leaves — monsteras are susceptible
Confirm the cutting or plant has been rooted before purchasing, unless you are buying intentionally unrooted — unrooted cuttings require a propagation setup to succeed

fieldNotes.title: More Air Than Leaf
fieldNotes.author: Aroid Aaron
fieldNotes.content: Monstera obliqua (Peru form) is the ultimate expression of botanical fenestration. With leaves that are up to 90% holes, it looks like a green spiderweb. In Michael Madison's classic 1977 monograph on the genus Monstera, obliqua was described as a widespread but rarely collected species, with the Peru form being the most extreme. The massive fenestrations are an evolutionary strategy to cover a larger surface area to capture light flecks on the forest floor without the energetic cost of building leaf tissue. It is notoriously fragile, demanding high humidity (over 80%) to prevent the delicate leaf threads from drying out.
